user_request: Draft a commercial suit seeking damages for illegal termination of dealership agreement. Plaintiff invested substantial capital and developed territory market. Termination was arbitrary and contrary to agreement terms. Claim compensation for loss of profit, goodwill and unsold stock. Draft with proper breach of contract pleading.

Denial of contract. All matters which go to show that the contract sued on is void or unlawful must be specifically 
pleaded. If such matters are not expressly pleaded, no evidence thereof can, as a rule, be given at the trial. Thus, if 
A sues B on a contract, and B in his written statement merely denies the contract, such denial will be taken to mean 
only that there was in fact no such contract as alleged: it will not be construed as a denial of the legality of the 
contract. The result is that if A proves the contract sued upon, B will not be allowed to contend at the hearing that 
the contract was a wagering contract, and, therefore, void. B ought to have specifically pleaded in his written 
statement that the contract was a wagering contract. 40 However, where at the hearing of a suit, the plaintiffs case 
discloses that the transaction which is the basis of his claim is illegal, the court cannot properly ignore the illegality, 
even if the illegality be not pleaded or relied on by the defendant. 41 No court ought to enforce an illegal contract or 
allow itself to be made the instrument of enforcing obligations alleged to arise out of a contract or transaction which 
is illegal, if the illegality is duly brought to the notice of the court, and if the person invoking the aid of the court is 
himself

as a denial in fact of the express contract or of the matters of fact from which the same may be implied, and not as 
a denial of the legality or sufficiency in law of such contract.

without setting out the whole or any part thereof, unless the precise words of the document or

Rule 10: Malice, Knowledge, etc - Wherever it is material to allege malice, fraudulent

inferred.

thing, it shall be sufficient to allege such notice as a fact, unless the form or the precise terms 
of such notice, or the circumstances from which such notice is to be inferred are material.

legal_research.summary:
[Statute of limitations in contract claims : an overview - iPleaders] Under normal circumstances, the Indian Limitation Act of 1963, under Section 14 of the Limitation Act, provides that a claim for breach of contract must be made within three years from the date of such breach .
[The Period of Limitation For A Suit For Compensation For The Breach of Any Contract | PDF | Breach Of Contract | Damages] It is three years from the date the contract was broken, or from the date of the most recent breach if there were successive breaches, or from the date the continuing breach ceased .
[Applicabilty of Limitation Act to Indian Contract Act] In other words, if there is no specific provision in the Limitation Act or any other law that prescribes a limitation period for a particular type of suit, then the plaintiff can file such a suit within three years from the date when the right ...
[1 Limitation Periods: Overview (India)] Limitation Act prescribes separate periods for the preference of suit in tort, and for breach of · obligations under contract.
[Dhapai vs Dalla And Ors. on 2 April, 1969] Three years When the contract is broken, or (where there are successive breaches) when the breach in respect of which the suit is instituted occurs or (where the breach is continuing) when it ceases . 120. Suit for which no period of limitation is provided elsewhere in this Schedule.
[The Plaint: Foundational Document in Civil Litigation under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] Order IV, Rule 1 of the CPC mandates that “ Every suit shall be instituted by presenting a plaint in duplicate to the Court or such officer as it appoints in this behalf ” and that “Every plaint shall comply with the rules contained in Orders ...
[Strict Compliance with Section 80 of the Civil Procedure Code for Suits Against the Government: Amar Nath Dogra v. Union of India: Supreme Court Of India | CaseMine] During the tenure of his license, Dogra alleged breaches of contractual stipulations by the Excise Authorities, leading him to serve a notice under Section 80 of the Civil Procedure Code (CPC) and subsequently file a suit claiming damages.
[Documents Filed with Plaint: Comprehensive Legal Framework and Judicial Interpretation - Bhatt & Joshi Associates] The judgment highlighted that allowing parties to introduce documents at later stages without proper justification would undermine the procedural integrity of civil suits and potentially prejudice the opposing party’s ability to prepare an adequate defense. The decision also clarified that the requirement to file documents with the plaint is not merely directory but mandatory, meaning that non-compliance carries substantive consequences.
[Revisiting The Principles Of Damages Under The Contract Regime In India - Contracts and Commercial Law - India] The Supreme Court in M.N. Gangappa v. Atmakur Nagabhushanam Shetty & Co. and Anr., (AIR 1972 SC 696) has observed that in absence of a strict procedure for calculation of damages arising out of breach of contract, damages would be calculated based on the facts and circumstances of each case.

lkb_brief.coa_guidance: The cause of action is a SINGLE EVENT — illegal termination. Do NOT plead 'continuing breach' because termination is a one-time act. Three-part CoA: (1) first arose on date of termination letter, (2) further arose when defendant failed to respond to protest/legal notice, (3) cause is NOT continuing — but defendant's retention of benefits of plaintiff's investment and market development continues, and plaintiff's right to damages subsists.
lkb_brief.interest_guidance: Defendant retained the benefit of plaintiff's capital investment and market development. Interest justified at commercial rate reflecting the reasonable return plaintiff would have earned on invested capital.

mandatory_inline_sections.instruction: Include a separate paragraph stating compliance with Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015: 'The Plaintiff states that in compliance with Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, pre-institution mediation was attempted/undertaken through {{MEDIATION_AUTHORITY}} but the same was unsuccessful / the Defendant failed to participate.' If urgent interim relief is sought, state: 'The Plaintiff seeks urgent interim relief and is therefore exempt from the requirement of pre-institution mediation under Section 12A.' This is MANDATORY for Commercial Court suits.

Include a STATEMENT OF TRUTH as required by the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. Format: 'STATEMENT OF TRUTH\n\nI, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff herein, do hereby state that the facts stated in paragraphs 1 to [last para] of this plaint are true to my knowledge and belief and that I have not wilfully suppressed any material fact. I understand that I may be liable for proceedings under the Indian Penal Code / Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita for making a false statement.' This is MANDATORY for Commercial Court suits.

Include a DETAILED DAMAGES SCHEDULE as required by the Commercial Courts Act. For each damages category, show: (a) head of damage, (b) amount claimed, (c) methodology/basis for computation, (d) documentary proof reference. Cover: loss of profit (projected from historical data), goodwill (based on territory value, brand equity, customer base), wasted capital (itemised with invoices/receipts), unsold stock (at cost or market value, whichever lower).

draft_artifacts.title: Commercial Suit No. __________ / 20____
draft_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE COMMERCIAL COURT (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

Commercial Suit No. __________ / 20____

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}},
Aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Carrying on business as Authorized Dealer under the name and style of {{BUSINESS_NAME}},
Having registered office at: {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

{{DEFENDANT_NAME}},
A company incorporated under the Companies Act, 2013,
Having its registered office at: {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}},
Through its Authorized Representative.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

COMMERCIAL SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF DAMAGES OF RS. {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/- WITH INTEREST AND COSTS FOR ILLEGAL TERMINATION OF DEALERSHIP AGREEMENT

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Plaintiff is an authorized dealer engaged in the business of distribution and sale of {{PRODUCT_DESCRIPTION}} within the territory of {{TERRITORY_DESCRIPTION}}. The Defendant is a company incorporated under the Companies Act, 2013 and is engaged in the business of manufacture and supply of {{PRODUCT_DESCRIPTION}}.

2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the Defendant carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which falls within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court. Furthermore, the dealership agreement was executed at {{AGREEMENT_EXECUTION_PLACE}}, the Plaintiff's business premises are situated at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, and the cause of action arose within the territorial limits of this Hon'ble Court.

3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Commercial Court. This Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain commercial suits of this nature under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

4. The Plaintiff states that in compliance with Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, pre-institution mediation was undertaken through {{MEDIATION_AUTHORITY}} vide Application No. {{MEDIATION_APPLICATION_NUMBER}} dated {{MEDIATION_APPLICATION_DATE}}. The mediation proceedings were conducted on {{MEDIATION_SESSION_DATE}}. However, the mediation was unsuccessful as the Defendant failed to participate constructively and did not offer any reasonable settlement. The mediation was concluded as unsuccessful on {{MEDIATION_FAILURE_DATE}}. A true copy of the mediation application, mediation proceedings record, and the unsuccessful mediation report are annexed hereto and marked as Annexure A.

FACTS OF THE CASE

5. The Plaintiff is an established businessman carrying on the business of distribution and sale of {{PRODUCT_DESCRIPTION}} under the name and style of {{BUSINESS_NAME}} at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}} for the past {{YEARS_IN_BUSINESS}} years. The Plaintiff has developed substantial expertise, infrastructure, and market presence in the distribution and retail of {{PRODUCT_DESCRIPTION}} within the assigned territory.

6. The Defendant is a leading manufacturer and supplier of {{PRODUCT_DESCRIPTION}} having its registered office at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}. The Defendant appointed the Plaintiff as an authorized dealer for the territory of {{TERRITORY_DESCRIPTION}} vide Dealership Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} executed at {{AGREEMENT_EXECUTION_PLACE}} (a true copy of the Dealership Agreement is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure B).

7. The Dealership Agreement was valid for a period of {{AGREEMENT_DURATION}} years commencing from {{AGREEMENT_COMMENCEMENT_DATE}} and was to expire on {{AGREEMENT_EXPIRY_DATE}}. Clause {{CLAUSE_NUMBER}} of the Dealership Agreement provided that the agreement could be terminated by either party by giving {{NOTICE_PERIOD}} months' written notice to the other party. Clause {{CLAUSE_NUMBER}} further provided that in the event of any alleged breach by the dealer, the manufacturer shall first issue a written notice specifying the breach and grant an opportunity of {{CURE_PERIOD}} days to cure the breach before proceeding with termination.

8. In pursuance of the Dealership Agreement and in good faith reliance upon the same, the Plaintiff made substantial capital investment for development of the dealership business. The Plaintiff invested approximately Rs. {{CAPITAL_INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}}/- towards establishment of showroom, warehouse, storage facilities, delivery vehicles, computer systems, and other infrastructure as required by the Defendant's specifications. The Plaintiff also employed {{NUMBER_OF_EMPLOYEES}} persons for managing the dealership operations. The details of capital investment with supporting invoices and receipts are enumerated in the Particulars of Damage annexed to this plaint.

9. Over the period of the dealership, the Plaintiff diligently developed the territory market for the Defendant's products. The Plaintiff established a network of {{NUMBER_OF_SUB_DEALERS}} sub-dealers and retail outlets within the territory. The Plaintiff undertook extensive marketing activities, promotional campaigns, and customer service initiatives which resulted in substantial growth in sales volumes. The annual sales turnover increased from Rs. {{INITIAL_SALES_TURNOVER}}/- in the first year to Rs. {{FINAL_SALES_TURNOVER}}/- in the most recent year. The Plaintiff's performance was consistently rated as {{PERFORMANCE_RATING}} by the Defendant's own evaluation system.

10. On or about {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}}, the Defendant issued a termination letter purporting to terminate the Dealership Agreement with immediate effect, citing alleged non-performance and breach of certain terms of the agreement (a true copy of the termination letter is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure C). The termination was issued without providing any prior notice as mandated under Clause {{CLAUSE_NUMBER}} of the Dealership Agreement. The termination letter did not specify any particulars of the alleged breach and did not afford the Plaintiff any opportunity to cure the alleged breach as contractually required under Clause {{CLAUSE_NUMBER}}.

11. The Plaintiff immediately responded to the termination letter vide communication dated {{PLAINTIFF_RESPONSE_DATE}} denying the allegations of non-performance and breach, pointing out that the termination was in violation of the contractual terms, and requesting the Defendant to withdraw the termination and restore the dealership (a true copy of the Plaintiff's response is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure D). The Plaintiff specifically highlighted that the termination was arbitrary, without any factual basis, and contrary to the express terms of the Dealership Agreement.

12. The Defendant, vide its reply dated {{DEFENDANT_REPLY_DATE}}, reiterated the termination without addressing the Plaintiff's contentions regarding violation of contractual procedure (a true copy of the Defendant's reply is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure E). The Defendant failed to provide any documentary evidence or particulars of the alleged breach. The Defendant also failed to explain why the mandatory notice period and cure period were not provided before termination.

13. The Plaintiff, through his advocate, caused a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} to be served upon the Defendant calling upon the Defendant to withdraw the illegal termination, restore the dealership, and compensate the Plaintiff for losses suffered (a true copy of the legal notice along with postal receipt and acknowledgment is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure F). The Defendant's reply to the legal notice dated {{DEFENDANT_NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} was evasive and did not offer any meaningful response (a true copy is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure G).

14. As a direct consequence of the illegal termination, the Plaintiff has suffered substantial losses. The Plaintiff is left with unsold stock of the Defendant's products valued at Rs. {{UNSOLD_STOCK_VALUE}}/- which cannot be returned or sold at reasonable prices. The Plaintiff's capital investment of Rs. {{CAPITAL_INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}}/- in infrastructure specifically created for the Defendant's dealership has been rendered wasted. The Plaintiff has lost the goodwill and market position developed over {{DEALERSHIP_DURATION}} years, valued at Rs. {{GOODWILL_VALUE}}/-. The Plaintiff has lost the profit that would have been earned during the unexpired term of the agreement, estimated at Rs. {{LOSS_OF_PROFIT_AMOUNT}}/-. The detailed computation of damages is provided in the Particulars of Damage annexed to this plaint.

15. The Plaintiff submits that the termination was illegal and contrary to the express terms of the Dealership Agreement for the following reasons: (a) no prior written notice of {{NOTICE_PERIOD}} months was given as required under Clause {{CLAUSE_NUMBER}}; (b) no opportunity to cure the alleged breach was provided as mandated under Clause {{CLAUSE_NUMBER}}; (c) no particulars of the alleged breach were specified in the termination letter; (d) the Plaintiff was not in breach of any term of the agreement; (e) the termination was arbitrary and motivated by the Defendant's desire to appoint another dealer on more favourable terms, as evidenced by the fact that the Defendant appointed {{NEW_DEALER_NAME}} as dealer for the same territory within {{TIME_PERIOD}} days of terminating the Plaintiff's dealership.

16. The Plaintiff submits that there was no oral variation or modification of the Dealership Agreement. The Defendant cannot claim that any oral understanding superseded the written terms of the agreement. The terms of the written agreement regarding notice period and cure period remained binding and the Defendant was obliged to comply with the same before terminating the dealership.

17. The Plaintiff further submits that the Defendant has retained the benefit of the Plaintiff's substantial capital investment and market development efforts. The infrastructure created by the Plaintiff, the customer base developed by the Plaintiff, and the market presence established by the Plaintiff over {{DEALERSHIP_DURATION}} years have all been appropriated by the Defendant for the benefit of the newly appointed dealer. The Defendant cannot be permitted to benefit from its own wrongful act.

LEGAL BASIS

18. The Plaintiff submits that the Defendant is liable under Section 39 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 which provides that when a party to a contract has refused to perform or disabled himself from performing his promise in its entirety, the promisee may put an end to the contract. In the present case, the Defendant has wrongfully repudiated the Dealership Agreement by issuing an illegal termination letter without complying with the contractual terms. The Plaintiff is entitled to treat the contract as rescinded by the Defendant's breach and claim damages for the loss suffered.

19. The Plaintiff further submits that the Defendant is liable under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 which provides that when a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive from the party who has broken the contract compensation for any loss or damage caused thereby which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach or which the parties knew when they made the contract to be likely to result from the breach. Section 73 further provides that such compensation is not to be given for any remote or indirect loss or damage sustained by reason of the breach. In the present case, the losses claimed by the Plaintiff, namely loss of profit for the unexpired term, loss of goodwill, wasted capital investment, and loss on unsold stock, are all direct and natural consequences of the Defendant's illegal termination and were within the contemplation of the parties when the Dealership Agreement was entered into.

20. Without prejudice to the above and in the alternative, the Plaintiff submits that the Defendant is also liable under Section 74 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 which provides that when a contract has been broken, if a sum is named in the contract as the amount to be paid in case of such breach, the party complaining of the breach is entitled, whether or not actual damage or loss is proved to have been caused thereby, to receive from the party who has broken the contract reasonable compensation not exceeding the amount so named. The Dealership Agreement contained a stipulation regarding compensation in case of termination. Even otherwise, the Plaintiff is entitled to reasonable compensation for the breach.

21. The Plaintiff submits that the Defendant's termination was in breach of the express terms of the Dealership Agreement regarding notice period and cure period. The Defendant cannot rely on any alleged oral variation of the agreement. The terms of the written agreement remained binding and enforceable. The Defendant's failure to provide the contractually mandated notice period and cure period constitutes a material breach of the agreement entitling the Plaintiff to damages.

22. The Plaintiff further submits that the Defendant was under an obligation to act in good faith in the performance and termination of the contract. The arbitrary termination without any factual basis, without following contractual procedure, and with the ulterior motive of appointing another dealer constitutes a breach of the implied duty of good faith. The Defendant cannot be permitted to benefit from the Plaintiff's investment and market development efforts while denying the Plaintiff the opportunity to continue the dealership for the agreed term.

CAUSE OF ACTION

23. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}} when the Defendant issued the termination letter purporting to terminate the Dealership Agreement with immediate effect, without complying with the contractual requirements of notice period and cure period, and without any valid ground for termination, thereby committing a fundamental breach of the agreement and giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to claim damages.

24. The cause of action further arose on {{PLAINTIFF_RESPONSE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite the Plaintiff's protest and request to withdraw the illegal termination, failed and refused to withdraw the termination or provide any justification for the same. The cause of action further arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice, failed to comply with the demands therein. The cause of action further arose on {{DEFENDANT_NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} when the Defendant sent an evasive reply to the legal notice without offering any reasonable response.

25. The cause of action is not a continuing cause of action. The breach was a single event, namely the illegal termination dated {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}}. However, the Defendant continues to retain the benefit of the Plaintiff's capital investment and market development efforts, and the Plaintiff's right to damages subsists.

LIMITATION

26. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years for compensation for breach of any contract, express or implied. The cause of action having arisen on {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}} when the Defendant committed the breach by issuing the illegal termination letter, and the suit being filed within three years from the said date, the present suit is well within limitation.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

27. The suit is valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee, being the aggregate of the damages claimed. The requisite court fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}/- has been paid on this plaint in accordance with the applicable Court Fees Act, computed on the suit valuation at the applicable ad valorem rate.

INTEREST

28. The Plaintiff claims interest on the damages amount as follows, the Defendant having wrongfully terminated the dealership and deprived the Plaintiff of the use of his invested capital and expected returns:

    (a) Pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}} (the date of illegal termination) till the date of filing of this suit, the said rate being justified on the ground that the Defendant has retained the benefit of the Plaintiff's capital investment which the Plaintiff could have otherwise deployed in his business at prevailing commercial rates;

    (b) Pendente lite interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum or at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just and reasonable during the pendency of this suit under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;

    (c) Future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till the date of actual realization.

PRAYER

29. In the premises stated hereinabove, it is most respectfully prayed that this Hon'ble Court may be graciously pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for damages of Rs. {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) being the aggregate of loss of profit for the unexpired term of Rs. {{LOSS_OF_PROFIT_AMOUNT}}/-, loss of goodwill of Rs. {{GOODWILL_VALUE}}/-, wasted capital investment of Rs. {{CAPITAL_INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}}/-, and loss on unsold stock of Rs. {{UNSOLD_STOCK_VALUE}}/-;

    (b) Award pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum on the said sum from {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}} till the date of filing of this suit;

    (c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just and reasonable during the pendency of this suit;

    (d) Award future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till the date of actual realization;

    (e) Award costs of this suit including court fees, advocate fees, and all pre-litigation costs incurred for mediation and issuing the legal notice;

    (f) Grant such other and further relief as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

AND FOR THIS ACT OF KINDNESS, THE PLAINTIFF SHALL, AS IN DUTY BOUND, EVER PRAY.

PARTICULARS OF DAMAGES

The Plaintiff claims damages under the following heads with detailed methodology and supporting documents:

A. LOSS OF PROFIT FOR UNEXPIRED TERM

Amount Claimed: Rs. {{LOSS_OF_PROFIT_AMOUNT}}/-

Methodology: The loss of profit has been computed based on the average annual profit earned by the Plaintiff during the preceding {{NUMBER_OF_YEARS}} years of the dealership. The average annual profit was Rs. {{AVERAGE_ANNUAL_PROFIT}}/-. The unexpired term of the agreement was {{UNEXPIRED_TERM}} months. The loss of profit is computed as the average annual profit multiplied by the unexpired term, adjusted for projected growth based on historical trends.

Supporting Documents:
- Audited financial statements for the past {{NUMBER_OF_YEARS}} years (Annexure H)
- Sales records and profit margins (Annexure I)
- Growth projections and market analysis (Annexure J)

B. LOSS OF GOODWILL

Amount Claimed: Rs. {{GOODWILL_VALUE}}/-

Methodology: The goodwill has been valued based on the capitalization of super profits method. The Plaintiff had developed substantial goodwill in the territory through years of consistent service, customer relationships, and brand building. The goodwill is computed as the present value of future super profits attributable to the dealership, discounted at the prevailing commercial rate. Alternatively, the goodwill is computed based on the number of years of purchase of average profits at {{YEARS_OF_PURCHASE}} years.

Supporting Documents:
- Customer database and relationship records (Annexure K)
- Market survey reports establishing brand recognition (Annexure L)
- Goodwill valuation report by certified valuer (Annexure M)

C. WASTED CAPITAL INVESTMENT

Amount Claimed: Rs. {{CAPITAL_INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}}/-

Methodology: The capital investment made specifically for the Defendant's dealership has been rendered wasted due to the illegal termination. The investment includes showroom construction/renovation, warehouse and storage facilities, delivery vehicles, computer systems and software, signage and branding, and other infrastructure as required by the Defendant's specifications. The written down value of these assets as on the date of termination is claimed.

Supporting Documents:
- Itemized list of capital investments with invoices and receipts (Annexure N)
- Bank statements showing payment for investments (Annexure O)
- Photographs of showroom and infrastructure (Annexure P)
- Depreciation schedule and written down value computation (Annexure Q)

D. LOSS ON UNSOLD STOCK

Amount Claimed: Rs. {{UNSOLD_STOCK_VALUE}}/-

Methodology: The Plaintiff was left with unsold stock of the Defendant's products at the time of illegal termination. The stock has been valued at cost or market value, whichever is lower, in accordance with sound accounting principles. The Defendant has refused to take back the unsold stock or compensate the Plaintiff for the same. The stock is now difficult to sell at reasonable prices due to the termination of the dealership.

Supporting Documents:
- Stock statement as on date of termination (Annexure R)
- Purchase invoices for the unsold stock (Annexure S)
- Valuation of stock at cost and market value (Annexure T)

E. FUTURE BUSINESS OPPORTUNITIES

Amount Claimed: Rs. {{FUTURE_OPPORTUNITIES_AMOUNT}}/-

Methodology: The Plaintiff had developed substantial business relationships and was in the process of expanding the dealership network within the territory. The illegal termination has resulted in loss of these future business opportunities. The amount is computed based on the projected revenue from identified business opportunities that were in advanced stages of negotiation.

Supporting Documents:
- Correspondence with prospective sub-dealers and customers (Annexure U)
- Projected revenue from identified opportunities (Annexure V)

TOTAL DAMAGES CLAIMED: Rs. {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/-
(Rupees {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only)

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

| Sr. No. | Description | Annexure |

|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1. | Mediation application, proceedings record, and unsuccessful mediation report | Annexure A |
| 2. | Dealership Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} | Annexure B |
| 3. | Termination letter dated {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}} | Annexure C |
| 4. | Plaintiff's response dated {{PLAINTIFF_RESPONSE_DATE}} | Annexure D |
| 5. | Defendant's reply dated {{DEFENDANT_REPLY_DATE}} | Annexure E |
| 6. | Legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} with postal receipt and acknowledgment | Annexure F |
| 7. | Defendant's reply to legal notice dated {{DEFENDANT_NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} | Annexure G |
| 8. | Audited financial statements for past years | Annexure H |
| 9. | Sales records and profit margins | Annexure I |
| 10. | Growth projections and market analysis | Annexure J |
| 11. | Customer database and relationship records | Annexure K |
| 12. | Market survey reports | Annexure L |
| 13. | Goodwill valuation report | Annexure M |
| 14. | Itemized list of capital investments with invoices | Annexure N |
| 15. | Bank statements for investments | Annexure O |
| 16. | Photographs of showroom and infrastructure | Annexure P |
| 17. | Depreciation schedule | Annexure Q |
| 18. | Stock statement as on termination date | Annexure R |
| 19. | Purchase invoices for unsold stock | Annexure S |
| 20. | Stock valuation statement | Annexure T |
| 21. | Correspondence with prospective sub-dealers | Annexure U |
| 22. | Projected revenue from identified opportunities | Annexure V |

VERIFICATION

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby solemnly verify and state on oath that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 29 of this plaint are true and correct to my personal knowledge based upon the records and documents available with me, that the contents relating to legal submissions are based upon legal advice received and are believed to be correct, that no part of the cause of action has arisen within the jurisdiction of any other court, and that nothing material has been concealed therefrom. Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

                                                        {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

                                                        PLAINTIFF

STATEMENT OF TRUTH

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff herein, do hereby state that the facts stated in paragraphs 1 to 29 of this plaint are true to my knowledge and belief and that I have not wilfully suppressed any material fact. I understand that I may be liable for proceedings under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 for making a false statement.

                                                        {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

                                                        PLAINTIFF

Through:

Through its Authorized Representative.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

COMMERCIAL SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF DAMAGES OF RS. {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/- WITH INTEREST AND COSTS FOR ILLEGAL TERMINATION OF DEALERSHIP AGREEMENT

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Plaintiff is an authorized dealer engaged in the business of distribution and sale of {{PRODUCT_DESCRIPTION}} within the territory of {{TERRITORY_DESCRIPTION}}. The Defendant is a company incorporated under the Companies Act, 2013 and is engaged in the business of manufacture and supply of {{PRODUCT_DESCRIPTION}}.

2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the Defendant carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which falls within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court. Furthermore, the dealership agreement was executed at {{AGREEMENT_EXECUTION_PLACE}}, the Plaintiff's business premises are situated at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}, and the cause of action arose within the territorial limits of this Hon'ble Court.

3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Commercial Court. This Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain commercial suits of this nature under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

4. The Plaintiff states that in compliance with Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, pre-institution mediation was undertaken through {{MEDIATION_AUTHORITY}} vide Application No. {{MEDIATION_APPLICATION_NUMBER}} dated {{MEDIATION_APPLICATION_DATE}}. The mediation proceedings were conducted on {{MEDIATION_SESSION_DATE}}. However, the mediation was unsuccessful as the Defendant failed to participate constructively and did not offer any reasonable settlement. The mediation was concluded as unsuccessful on {{MEDIATION_FAILURE_DATE}}. A true copy of the mediation application, mediation proceedings record, and the unsuccessful mediation report are annexed hereto and marked as Annexure A.

FACTS OF THE CASE

5. The Plaintiff is an established businessman carrying on the business of distribution and sale of {{PRODUCT_DESCRIPTION}} under the name and style of {{BUSINESS_NAME}} at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}} for the past {{YEARS_IN_BUSINESS}} years. The Plaintiff has developed substantial expertise, infrastructure, and market presence in the distribution and retail of {{PRODUCT_DESCRIPTION}} within the assigned territory.

6. The Defendant is a leading manufacturer and supplier of {{PRODUCT_DESCRIPTION}} having its registered office at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}. The Defendant appointed the Plaintiff as an authorized dealer for the territory of {{TERRITORY_DESCRIPTION}} vide Dealership Agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} executed at {{AGREEMENT_EXECUTION_PLACE}} (a true copy of the Dealership Agreement is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure B).

7. The Dealership Agreement was valid for a period of {{AGREEMENT_DURATION}} years commencing from {{AGREEMENT_COMMENCEMENT_DATE}} and was to expire on {{AGREEMENT_EXPIRY_DATE}}. Clause {{CLAUSE_NUMBER}} of the Dealership Agreement provided that the agreement could be terminated by either party by giving {{NOTICE_PERIOD}} months' written notice to the other party. Clause {{CLAUSE_NUMBER}} further provided that in the event of any alleged breach by the dealer, the manufacturer shall first issue a written notice specifying the breach and grant an opportunity of {{CURE_PERIOD}} days to cure the breach before proceeding with termination.

8. In pursuance of the Dealership Agreement and in good faith reliance upon the same, the Plaintiff made substantial capital investment for development of the dealership business. The Plaintiff invested approximately Rs. {{CAPITAL_INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}}/- towards establishment of showroom, warehouse, storage facilities, delivery vehicles, computer systems, and other infrastructure as required by the Defendant's specifications. The Plaintiff also employed {{NUMBER_OF_EMPLOYEES}} persons for managing the dealership operations. The details of capital investment with supporting invoices and receipts are enumerated in the Particulars of Damage annexed to this plaint.

9. Over the period of the dealership, the Plaintiff diligently developed the territory market for the Defendant's products. The Plaintiff established a network of {{NUMBER_OF_SUB_DEALERS}} sub-dealers and retail outlets within the territory. The Plaintiff undertook extensive marketing activities, promotional campaigns, and customer service initiatives which resulted in substantial growth in sales volumes. The annual sales turnover increased from Rs. {{INITIAL_SALES_TURNOVER}}/- in the first year to Rs. {{FINAL_SALES_TURNOVER}}/- in the most recent year. The Plaintiff's performance was consistently rated as {{PERFORMANCE_RATING}} by the Defendant's own evaluation system.

10. On or about {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}}, the Defendant issued a termination letter purporting to terminate the Dealership Agreement with immediate effect, citing alleged non-performance and breach of certain terms of the agreement (a true copy of the termination letter is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure C). The termination was issued without providing any prior notice as mandated under Clause {{CLAUSE_NUMBER}} of the Dealership Agreement. The termination letter did not specify any particulars of the alleged breach and did not afford the Plaintiff any opportunity to cure the alleged breach as contractually required under Clause {{CLAUSE_NUMBER}}.

11. The Plaintiff immediately responded to the termination letter vide communication dated {{PLAINTIFF_RESPONSE_DATE}} denying the allegations of non-performance and breach, pointing out that the termination was in violation of the contractual terms, and requesting the Defendant to withdraw the termination and restore the dealership (a true copy of the Plaintiff's response is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure D). The Plaintiff specifically highlighted that the termination was arbitrary, without any factual basis, and contrary to the express terms of the Dealership Agreement.

12. The Defendant, vide its reply dated {{DEFENDANT_REPLY_DATE}}, reiterated the termination without addressing the Plaintiff's contentions regarding violation of contractual procedure (a true copy of the Defendant's reply is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure E). The Defendant failed to provide any documentary evidence or particulars of the alleged breach. The Defendant also failed to explain why the mandatory notice period and cure period were not provided before termination.

13. The Plaintiff, through his advocate, caused a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} to be served upon the Defendant calling upon the Defendant to withdraw the illegal termination, restore the dealership, and compensate the Plaintiff for losses suffered (a true copy of the legal notice along with postal receipt and acknowledgment is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure F). The Defendant's reply to the legal notice dated {{DEFENDANT_NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} was evasive and did not offer any meaningful response (a true copy is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure G).

14. As a direct consequence of the illegal termination, the Plaintiff has suffered substantial losses. The Plaintiff is left with unsold stock of the Defendant's products valued at Rs. {{UNSOLD_STOCK_VALUE}}/- which cannot be returned or sold at reasonable prices. The Plaintiff's capital investment of Rs. {{CAPITAL_INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}}/- in infrastructure specifically created for the Defendant's dealership has been rendered wasted. The Plaintiff has lost the goodwill and market position developed over {{DEALERSHIP_DURATION}} years, valued at Rs. {{GOODWILL_VALUE}}/-. The Plaintiff has lost the profit that would have been earned during the unexpired term of the agreement, estimated at Rs. {{LOSS_OF_PROFIT_AMOUNT}}/-. The detailed computation of damages is provided in the Particulars of Damage annexed to this plaint.

15. The Plaintiff submits that the termination was illegal and contrary to the express terms of the Dealership Agreement for the following reasons: (a) no prior written notice of {{NOTICE_PERIOD}} months was given as required under Clause {{CLAUSE_NUMBER}}; (b) no opportunity to cure the alleged breach was provided as mandated under Clause {{CLAUSE_NUMBER}}; (c) no particulars of the alleged breach were specified in the termination letter; (d) the Plaintiff was not in breach of any term of the agreement; (e) the termination was arbitrary and motivated by the Defendant's desire to appoint another dealer on more favourable terms, as evidenced by the fact that the Defendant appointed {{NEW_DEALER_NAME}} as dealer for the same territory within {{TIME_PERIOD}} days of terminating the Plaintiff's dealership.

16. The Plaintiff submits that there was no oral variation or modification of the Dealership Agreement. The Defendant cannot claim that any oral understanding superseded the written terms of the agreement. The terms of the written agreement regarding notice period and cure period remained binding and the Defendant was obliged to comply with the same before terminating the dealership.

17. The Plaintiff further submits that the Defendant has retained the benefit of the Plaintiff's substantial capital investment and market development efforts. The infrastructure created by the Plaintiff, the customer base developed by the Plaintiff, and the market presence established by the Plaintiff over {{DEALERSHIP_DURATION}} years have all been appropriated by the Defendant for the benefit of the newly appointed dealer. The Defendant cannot be permitted to benefit from its own wrongful act.

LEGAL BASIS

18. The Plaintiff submits that the Defendant is liable under Section 39 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 which provides that when a party to a contract has refused to perform or disabled himself from performing his promise in its entirety, the promisee may put an end to the contract. In the present case, the Defendant has wrongfully repudiated the Dealership Agreement by issuing an illegal termination letter without complying with the contractual terms. The Plaintiff is entitled to treat the contract as rescinded by the Defendant's breach and claim damages for the loss suffered.

19. The Plaintiff further submits that the Defendant is liable under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 which provides that when a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive from the party who has broken the contract compensation for any loss or damage caused thereby which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach or which the parties knew when they made the contract to be likely to result from the breach. Section 73 further provides that such compensation is not to be given for any remote or indirect loss or damage sustained by reason of the breach. In the present case, the losses claimed by the Plaintiff, namely loss of profit for the unexpired term, loss of goodwill, wasted capital investment, and loss on unsold stock, are all direct and natural consequences of the Defendant's illegal termination and were within the contemplation of the parties when the Dealership Agreement was entered into.

20. Without prejudice to the above and in the alternative, the Plaintiff submits that the Defendant is also liable under Section 74 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 which provides that when a contract has been broken, if a sum is named in the contract as the amount to be paid in case of such breach, the party complaining of the breach is entitled, whether or not actual damage or loss is proved to have been caused thereby, to receive from the party who has broken the contract reasonable compensation not exceeding the amount so named. The Dealership Agreement contained a stipulation regarding compensation in case of termination. Even otherwise, the Plaintiff is entitled to reasonable compensation for the breach.

21. The Plaintiff submits that the Defendant's termination was in breach of the express terms of the Dealership Agreement regarding notice period and cure period. The Defendant cannot rely on any alleged oral variation of the agreement. The terms of the written agreement remained binding and enforceable. The Defendant's failure to provide the contractually mandated notice period and cure period constitutes a material breach of the agreement entitling the Plaintiff to damages.

22. The Plaintiff further submits that the Defendant was under an obligation to act in good faith in the performance and termination of the contract. The arbitrary termination without any factual basis, without following contractual procedure, and with the ulterior motive of appointing another dealer constitutes a breach of the implied duty of good faith. The Defendant cannot be permitted to benefit from the Plaintiff's investment and market development efforts while denying the Plaintiff the opportunity to continue the dealership for the agreed term.

CAUSE OF ACTION

23. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}} when the Defendant issued the termination letter purporting to terminate the Dealership Agreement with immediate effect, without complying with the contractual requirements of notice period and cure period, and without any valid ground for termination, thereby committing a fundamental breach of the agreement and giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to claim damages.

24. The cause of action further arose on {{PLAINTIFF_RESPONSE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite the Plaintiff's protest and request to withdraw the illegal termination, failed and refused to withdraw the termination or provide any justification for the same. The cause of action further arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice, failed to comply with the demands therein. The cause of action further arose on {{DEFENDANT_NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} when the Defendant sent an evasive reply to the legal notice without offering any reasonable response.

25. The cause of action is not a continuing cause of action. The breach was a single event, namely the illegal termination dated {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}}. However, the Defendant continues to retain the benefit of the Plaintiff's capital investment and market development efforts, and the Plaintiff's right to damages subsists.

LIMITATION

26. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years for compensation for breach of any contract, express or implied. The cause of action having arisen on {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}} when the Defendant committed the breach by issuing the illegal termination letter, and the suit being filed within three years from the said date, the present suit is well within limitation.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

27. The suit is valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee, being the aggregate of the damages claimed. The requisite court fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}/- has been paid on this plaint in accordance with the applicable Court Fees Act, computed on the suit valuation at the applicable ad valorem rate.

INTEREST

28. The Plaintiff claims interest on the damages amount as follows, the Defendant having wrongfully terminated the dealership and deprived the Plaintiff of the use of his invested capital and expected returns:

    (a) Pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}} (the date of illegal termination) till the date of filing of this suit, the said rate being justified on the ground that the Defendant has retained the benefit of the Plaintiff's capital investment which the Plaintiff could have otherwise deployed in his business at prevailing commercial rates;

    (b) Pendente lite interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum or at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just and reasonable during the pendency of this suit under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;

    (c) Future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till the date of actual realization.

PRAYER

29. In the premises stated hereinabove, it is most respectfully prayed that this Hon'ble Court may be graciously pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for damages of Rs. {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) being the aggregate of loss of profit for the unexpired term of Rs. {{LOSS_OF_PROFIT_AMOUNT}}/-, loss of goodwill of Rs. {{GOODWILL_VALUE}}/-, wasted capital investment of Rs. {{CAPITAL_INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}}/-, and loss on unsold stock of Rs. {{UNSOLD_STOCK_VALUE}}/-;

    (b) Award pre-suit interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum on the said sum from {{TERMINATION_LETTER_DATE}} till the date of filing of this suit;

    (c) Award pendente lite interest under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just and reasonable during the pendency of this suit;

    (d) Award future interest at the rate of {{INTEREST_RATE}}% per annum from the date of decree till the date of actual realization;

    (e) Award costs of this suit including court fees, advocate fees, and all pre-litigation costs incurred for mediation and issuing the legal notice;

    (f) Grant such other and further relief as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

AND FOR THIS ACT OF KINDNESS, THE PLAINTIFF SHALL, AS IN DUTY BOUND, EVER PRAY.

PARTICULARS OF DAMAGES

The Plaintiff claims damages under the following heads with detailed methodology and supporting documents:

A. LOSS OF PROFIT FOR UNEXPIRED TERM

Amount Claimed: Rs. {{LOSS_OF_PROFIT_AMOUNT}}/-

Methodology: The loss of profit has been computed based on the average annual profit earned by the Plaintiff during the preceding {{NUMBER_OF_YEARS}} years of the dealership. The average annual profit was Rs. {{AVERAGE_ANNUAL_PROFIT}}/-. The unexpired term of the agreement was {{UNEXPIRED_TERM}} months. The loss of profit is computed as the average annual profit multiplied by the unexpired term, adjusted for projected growth based on historical trends.

Supporting Documents:
- Audited financial statements for the past {{NUMBER_OF_YEARS}} years (Annexure H)
- Sales records and profit margins (Annexure I)
- Growth projections and market analysis (Annexure J)

B. LOSS OF GOODWILL

Amount Claimed: Rs. {{GOODWILL_VALUE}}/-

Methodology: The goodwill has been valued based on the capitalization of super profits method. The Plaintiff had developed substantial goodwill in the territory through years of consistent service, customer relationships, and brand building. The goodwill is computed as the present value of future super profits attributable to the dealership, discounted at the prevailing commercial rate. Alternatively, the goodwill is computed based on the number of years of purchase of average profits at {{YEARS_OF_PURCHASE}} years.

Supporting Documents:
- Customer database and relationship records (Annexure K)
- Market survey reports establishing brand recognition (Annexure L)
- Goodwill valuation report by certified valuer (Annexure M)

C. WASTED CAPITAL INVESTMENT

Amount Claimed: Rs. {{CAPITAL_INVESTMENT_AMOUNT}}/-

Methodology: The capital investment made specifically for the Defendant's dealership has been rendered wasted due to the illegal termination. The investment includes showroom construction/renovation, warehouse and storage facilities, delivery vehicles, computer systems and software, signage and branding, and other infrastructure as required by the Defendant's specifications. The written down value of these assets as on the date of termination is claimed.

Supporting Documents:
- Itemized list of capital investments with invoices and receipts (Annexure N)
- Bank statements showing payment for investments (Annexure O)
- Photographs of showroom and infrastructure (Annexure P)
- Depreciation schedule and written down value computation (Annexure Q)

D. LOSS ON UNSOLD STOCK

Amount Claimed: Rs. {{UNSOLD_STOCK_VALUE}}/-

Methodology: The Plaintiff was left with unsold stock of the Defendant's products at the time of illegal termination. The stock has been valued at cost or market value, whichever is lower, in accordance with sound accounting principles. The Defendant has refused to take back the unsold stock or compensate the Plaintiff for the same. The stock is now difficult to sell at reasonable prices due to the termination of the dealership.

Supporting Documents:
- Stock statement as on date of termination (Annexure R)
- Purchase invoices for the unsold stock (Annexure S)
- Valuation of stock at cost and market value (Annexure T)

E. FUTURE BUSINESS OPPORTUNITIES

Amount Claimed: Rs. {{FUTURE_OPPORTUNITIES_AMOUNT}}/-

Methodology: The Plaintiff had developed substantial business relationships and was in the process of expanding the dealership network within the territory. The illegal termination has resulted in loss of these future business opportunities. The amount is computed based on the projected revenue from identified business opportunities that were in advanced stages of negotiation.

Supporting Documents:
- Correspondence with prospective sub-dealers and customers (Annexure U)
- Projected revenue from identified opportunities (Annexure V)

TOTAL DAMAGES CLAIMED: Rs. {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT}}/-
(Rupees {{TOTAL_DAMAGES_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only)
